To: L. Hassan
From: PC
Subject: Tomorrow's status meeting — audit and recommended adjustments

L — you asked for observations before tomorrow. Five sections below, ordered by what I think you should decide before this evening.

1. MEETING PURPOSE — THE CONFLATION IS THE PROBLEM

The meeting as currently designed is two meetings stapled together. The first is an alignment meeting (weekly status — shared situational awareness, surface cross-workstream blockers). The second is a decision meeting (Stripe vs Adyen — a choice that needs to be made and owned). These are different meeting types with different right participants, different right durations, and different right preparation. The chapter's framing is direct: when status meetings are used to decide, either the meeting balloons or the decision is made poorly because the room is not set up for decision-making.

Specifically on this setup:
- The status meeting needs: delivery leads, recent status data, blocker triage. Pre-read should be one-page-per-workstream sent 24 hours ahead (the lapsed practice).
- The vendor decision needs: you (decider), M. Wong and A. Singh (technical input), E. Volkov (procurement input), V. Bauer if she actually wants in. Pre-read should be the technical evaluation + procurement findings + a 1-page comparison + your recommended call.

Putting them in the same room produces neither outcome well. Recommend: separate them.

2. AGENDA — EVERY ITEM IS A TOPIC LABEL, NOT AN OUTCOME

Each agenda item should be a sequence of outcomes with a time budget and a named owner. Current items vs proposed rewrites:

- "Status round-robin" → "Confirm each workstream's status against this week's milestones; surface cross-workstream blockers (25 min; leads brief in turn against the pre-read; blockers captured by PC for async resolution)."
- "Integration vendor discussion" → Move to a separate decision meeting. If kept here, it should read: "DECIDE: Stripe or Adyen (this is not an outcome that fits inside this meeting and should be removed)."
- "Q&A" → Drop. If a question needs to be raised, it happens during the status item. Standing Q&A items teach the room that questions are deferred.
- "AOB" → Drop. No AOB items have been raised in the last three meetings. Standing AOB slots that produce nothing waste 5 minutes per meeting × 52 meetings = ~4 hours of time on the calendar that returns no value.

3. INVITEES AND PRE-READ

Invitees (currently 14, the meeting will be poor with this many). My read:

KEEP (delivery leads who genuinely need cross-workstream awareness):
- L. Hassan (you), PC (me), M. Wong (Tech), D. Rivera (Back-end), A. Singh (Data), I. Hartman (QA), R. Silva (DevOps), K. Olafsson (Security), Y. Mbeki (Ops).

OPTIONAL or RECEIVE-MINUTES (the meeting is not currently serving them):
- S. Patel (Front-end) — has attended 1 of last 4. Worth a private check: is the meeting useful to them, or should they receive minutes?
- F. Chen (UX) — declining recently with no comment. Same question.

REMOVE FROM TOMORROW'S STATUS:
- V. Bauer (CDO / sponsor). Two reasons: she has not asked to attend, and putting her in the room changes what people say (workstream leads under-share concerns when the sponsor is observing). If you want her to have visibility, the right vehicle is the monthly steering committee or your 1:1 with her, not the weekly status.
- T. Adeyemi (programme director). If she finds value in attending fortnightly that is her call, but inviting her by default makes the meeting more formal than it needs to be. Worth a private question to her: does this serve you, or would minutes suffice?
- E. Volkov (procurement) — added for the vendor decision; should attend the separate decision meeting instead.

Pre-read: none has been sent for two months; the original one-pager-per-workstream practice lapsed. Recommend reinstating it for the next meeting — 24 hours ahead, owned by each workstream lead, max one page each. Without it, the first 15-20 minutes is briefing rather than discussion, and the meeting effectively starts 20 minutes late.

For the separate vendor decision meeting on (suggest) Thursday: pre-read should be the technical evaluation, procurement findings, a 1-page comparison from M. Wong / A. Singh / E. Volkov jointly, and your recommended call. 48 hours ahead (i.e. send today) so people can come prepared to confirm or push back.

4. FACILITATION PLAN FOR TOMORROW (if you keep tomorrow's meeting as currently configured)

Start on time at 10:00 sharp regardless of who is in. The room learns from your start time.

State the goal in the first minute: "We're here to confirm where each workstream is and surface cross-workstream blockers. The vendor decision is being moved to a focused meeting Thursday — we'll talk about that separately." This pre-empts the question and prevents the vendor topic from leaking into status.

For each lead's status update, hold to ~3 minutes; redirect if they drift into solutioning a blocker ("good — let's take that offline / capture as an action").

Surface dissent actively. R. Silva's multitasking and F. Chen's declines are signals of disengagement. If R. Silva is in the room tomorrow, ask him directly for his read on cross-workstream integration risk. Suppressed disengagement is more expensive than surfaced disengagement.

Close in the last two minutes with: decisions made (likely none for a status meeting — that's fine), actions agreed with owner and due date, follow-ups confirmed. Write the actions visibly. If we hit the half-hour mark with the cross-workstream conversation still going, end on time anyway and propose a focused follow-up — running over teaches the room that scheduled times are suggestions.

Respect the time budget. The original cadence was 30 minutes; we are at 60 because nobody pushed back. Tomorrow is a chance to land back at 30.

5. WHETHER THE MEETING SHOULD HAPPEN AS DESIGNED

My recommendation is: not as designed. The structural redesign I would land with the team:

A. Tomorrow: keep the slot but tighten to 30 minutes. Cover status only. Remove the vendor item from the agenda. Run the facilitation above. This is the immediate move.

B. This week: schedule a 45-minute vendor decision meeting on Thursday with the smaller decision group and a 48-hour pre-read going out today.

C. Going forward: reinstate the weekly pre-read (one-pager per workstream), trim attendance, hold the 30-minute slot. Re-evaluate at the end of the month — if attendance is still drifting after the trim, status moves to async (Slack thread or shared doc) and the slot becomes fortnightly cross-workstream working time.

I think this lands with the team as "getting back to the cadence we had" rather than as a critique of recent practice — that framing matters because the agenda creep happened with everyone's tacit agreement.

Two things I am NOT certain on and want your read: (a) whether V. Bauer's invitation tomorrow was your initiative or something she asked for — if the latter, the analysis above changes; (b) whether the Thursday vendor-decision meeting is realistic given your week — if not, Friday or early next week works as long as it lands before the 5-week integration sprint start.

PC
